title: 10 best herbal teas for nausea

teas.tea_desc: Ginger contains active compounds proven to alleviate digestive issues, including nausea. Specifically, its primary component, gingerol, neutralizes acid in the stomach and stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, promoting smooth digestion. Consequently, ginger tea effectively soothes queasiness, providing rapid relief. Additionally, this natural remedy has no known side effects, making it a popular and reliable choice for managing nausea.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: Ginger rhizomes are a powerful ingredient in medicinal tea for nausea. They contain phenolic compounds which are known to help reduce feelings of sickness and relax the gastrointestinal muscles.
Roots: Ginger roots are widely used in teas to alleviate nausea. They have anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the stomach and provide relief from queasiness.
Leaves: Ginger leaves are an excellent addition to medicinal tea for nausea. They are rich in antioxidants that can aid in digestion and reduce the symptoms of nausea.
Flowers: Ginger flowers are used in teas to help combat nausea. They contain active compounds that can help regulate the digestive system and provide quick relief from queasiness.
Seeds: Ginger seeds are sometimes included in medicinal tea for nausea. They have been found to have calming effects on the stomach and can help to alleviate the symptoms of nausea.
tea_recipe: Peel and grate 1 tablespoon of fresh ginger root.
In a teapot, combine grated ginger with 1 cup of water and boil for 10 minutes.
Strain the ginger-water mixture into a cup, discarding the solid ginger.
Add honey and lemon juice to taste, if desired.
Sip the ginger tea slowly while it is still warm to soothe nausea.
tea_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols are the main active components in ginger responsible for its potent anti-nausea effects. They work by blocking the release of serotonin, a chemical messenger that can trigger vomiting.

Shogaols: Shogaols are compounds formed when ginger is dried or cooked, and they have been found to be even more effective than gingerols in alleviating nausea. They work by relaxing the gastrointestinal tract and reducing inflammation.

Paradols: Paradols are anti-inflammatory compounds found in ginger that can help reduce nausea by inhibiting the production of prostaglandins, which are hormone-like substances that can cause inflammation and trigger nausea.

Zingerone: Zingerone is a phenolic compound in ginger that has been shown to have anti-emetic effects, meaning it can help reduce feelings of nausea and vomiting. It works by desensitizing receptors in the digestive tract that trigger nausea.

teas.tea_desc: Peppermint tea alleviates nausea due to its active components, particularly menthol. This compound relaxes the gastrointestinal tract, reducing symptoms of nausea. Furthermore, peppermint tea's soothing warmth stimulates the vagus nerve, which controls stomach emptying. Consequently, this process eases feelings of nausea. Additionally, peppermint tea's aroma activates the brain's satisfaction center, leading to a decrease in nausea sensation.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in menthol and menthone, peppermint leaves are well-known for soothing the stomach and alleviating nausea symptoms.
Flowers: High in volatile oils, peppermint flowers offer calming effects on the digestive system, helping to reduce nausea.
Stems: With similar properties to leaves, peppermint stems contribute to the tea's soothing effects on the stomach and aid in easing nausea.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 cup of boiling water with 1 tablespoon of dried peppermint leaves.
Steep the tea for 5-10 minutes to allow the peppermint leaves to release their oils.
Strain the tea to remove the used peppermint leaves.
Optionally, add honey or lemon to taste.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your stomach and alleviate nausea.
tea_constituents: Menthol: Menthol has anesthetic properties that help to relax the gastrointestinal tract, reducing the symptoms of nausea.
Mentone: Mentone is a volatile oil that helps to stimulate the stomach, improving digestion and alleviating feelings of nausea.
Limonene: Limonene is a terpene that helps to reduce inflammation in the stomach, providing relief from nausea caused by gastric inflammation.
Flavanones: Flavanones have antispasmodic properties that help to relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing the symptoms of nausea.
Eudesmol: Eudesmol is a sesquiterpene that has been shown to have anti-emetic properties, making it effective in reducing feelings of nausea.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea combats nausea due to its antiemetic properties, which come from its volatile oils like chamazulene and bisabolol. These compounds help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing tension and spasms that can cause nausea. Moreover, chamomile acts as a natural nervine, calming the nervous system and alleviating stress-induced nausea. Additionally, it contains flavonoids that act on the same receptors as anti-nausea drugs, further helping to combat feelings of queasiness. Lastly, chamomile's soothing warmth enhances gastric juices' flow, promoting healthy digestion and preventing nausea.
tea_parts: Flowers: Chamomile flowers are used in medicinal tea for nausea due to their calming properties that help soothe the stomach.
Leaves: The leaves of chamomile contain essential oils and antispasmodic properties that aid in reducing nausea.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds are rich in antioxidants and have carminative effects that help alleviate nausea and promote digestion.
tea_recipe: Boil 8 ounces of water to 212°F (100°C) for 1-2 minutes to release chamomile's calming properties.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers (or 1 chamomile tea bag) to the hot water.
Steep chamomile in the water for 5-10 minutes to extract its soothing compounds.
Strain or remove tea bag, discarding solids.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your mouth and soothe your stomach.
tea_constituents: Bisabolol: This anti-inflammatory compound can help reduce irritation in the stomach, helping to alleviate nausea.
Chamazulene: This aromatic compound has been found to help reduce inflammation in the digestive system, which can help ease nausea.
Apigenin: This antioxidant can help relax the stomach muscles and reduce tension, providing relief for nausea.
Matricin: This compound is converted to chamazulene in the steam-distillation process, and it has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce nausea.
Alpha-bisabolol oxide A and B: These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help soothe the stomach and reduce the feeling of nausea.

teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea relieves nausea due to its active component, glycyrrhizin, which possesses anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating properties. This compound stimulates the production of mucus in the gastrointestinal tract, protecting the stomach lining and reducing irritation. Moreover, glycyrrhizin inhibits the enzyme responsible for breaking down prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that regulate the contraction and relaxation of muscles in the stomach, thus decreasing the intensity and frequency of nausea. Additionally, licorice tea alleviates stress and anxiety, which often trigger nausea, by promoting relaxation and balancing cortisol levels. Lastly, this soothing beverage hydrates the body, replenishing essential fluids lost due to vomiting, further contributing to the reduction of nausea.
tea_parts: Roots: The underground root system of the licorice plant contains glycyrrhizin, a compound that helps soothe digestive system and alleviate nausea.
Rhyzomes: Similar to roots, the underground stem-like structures called rhyzomes of the licorice plant are rich in glycyrrhizin, providing relief from nausea.
Leaves: Although not as potent as roots and rhyzomes, the leaves of the licorice plant also contain beneficial compounds that can help reduce sensations of nausea.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the tea for 10-15 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse.
Strain the tea to remove the licorice root pieces.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your mouth and throat, and soothe your stomach.
tea_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound is the main active component in licorice and has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and anti-ulcer effects, which can help to reduce nausea caused by inflammation or stomach ulcers.
Liquiritin: This flavonoid has been found to have anti-emetic effects, meaning it can help to reduce feelings of nausea and vomiting.
Glabridin: This compound has been shown to have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help to reduce nausea caused by inflammation in the digestive tract.
isoliquiritigenin: This flavonoid has been found to have anti-spasmodic effects, which can help to reduce nausea caused by muscle spasms in the digestive tract.
Naringenin: This flavonoid has been shown to have anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help to reduce nausea caused by oxidative stress and inflammation in the digestive tract.

teas.tea_desc: Fennel tea provides relief from nausea due to its carminative and anti-emetic properties. This brew, derived from Foeniculum vulgare, contains volatile oils like anethole, fenchone, and estragole that relax gastrointestinal spasms. Fennel's active components accelerate gastric emptying, alleviating symptoms of nausea. Moreover, fennel tea's antispasmodic qualities soothe irritated mucous membranes in the digestive tract, further reducing sensations of queasiness. Lastly, clinical studies support fennel tea's ability to reduce the frequency and intensity of nausea, particularly in postoperative settings.
tea_parts: Seeds: Fennel seeds are excellent for making medicinal tea for nausea due to their carminative properties that help relieve digestive issues.
Leaves: The leaves of fennel contain volatile oils, which are beneficial in reducing nausea and promoting healthy digestion.
Stems: Fennel stems have calming properties that soothe the stomach, making them an ideal addition to a medicinal tea for nausea.
Flowers: The flowers of the fennel plant contain antispasmodic compounds, which are known to alleviate nausea and aid in digestion.
tea_recipe: Chop: Slice 1 tablespoon of fresh fennel seeds or 1 teaspoon of crushed fennel seeds.
Steep: Place chopped fennel in a tea infuser, and steep in a cup of boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain: Remove the infuser, and discard the used fennel.
Sweeten (optional): Stir in honey or sugar to taste, if desired.
Serve: Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day as needed to alleviate nausea.
tea_constituents: Anethole: Anethole has been shown to have antiemetic properties, meaning it can help reduce nausea and vomiting.
Fenchone: Fenchone has been found to have antispasmodic effects, which can help relax the gastrointestinal tract and alleviate nausea.
Estragole: Estragole has been suggested to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate nausea.
Limonene: Limonene has been shown to have calming effects and can help reduce stress and anxiety, which can contribute to nausea.
Alpha-phellandrene: Alpha-phellandrene has been found to have pain-relieving properties, which can help reduce discomfort and nausea caused by gastrointestinal issues.

teas.tea_desc: Raspberry tea's tangy taste and soothing warmth help calm upset stomachs, while its high concentration of antioxidants reduces inflammation in the digestive tract. Furthermore, this tea's tannins neutralize harmful free radicals that cause physical discomfort, providing immediate relief from nausea. Additionally, the tea's hydrating properties replenish essential fluids lost due to vomiting, restoring balance within the body.
tea_parts: Roots: Contain concentrated compounds that can alleviate nausea by calming the digestive system.
Leaves: Rich in soothing properties, providing relief from nausea symptoms.
Flowers: Known for their potent calming effects, reducing nausea and promoting overall digestive comfort.
Seeds: Packed with whole-body benefits, these tiny parts can effectively manage nausea symptoms.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried raspberry leaf with 1 cup of boiling water; steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the raspberry leaves, leaving only the liquid.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste for flavor.
Let the tea cool down to a comfortable drinking temperature.
Sip the raspberry tea slowly to alleviate nausea.
tea_constituents: Tannins: These astringent compounds can help to soothe an upset stomach and reduce inflammation, providing relief from nausea.
Flavonoids: Certain flavonoids, such as quercetin, have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help to alleviate nausea and vomiting.
Vitamin C: This essential vitamin can help to boost the immune system and reduce inflammation, which can help to alleviate nausea and other gastrointestinal symptoms.
Ellagic acid: This polyphenol antioxidant can help to protect the lining of the stomach and reduce inflammation, providing relief from nausea and other gastrointestinal symptoms.
Anthocyanins: These antioxidant compounds can help to reduce inflammation and provide relief from nausea, as well as improve digestion and absorption of nutrients.

teas.tea_desc: Slippery elm tea combats nausea through its powerful demulcent properties, coating the lining of the stomach and easing irritation. This soothing action reduces inflammation, creates a protective barrier, and encourages healing of any stomach lining damage causing nausea. Furthermore, it promotes mucus production, which lubricates the gastrointestinal tract, further alleviating symptoms. Ultimately, these mechanisms of action work together to provide relief from nausea.
tea_parts: Bark: The inner bark of the slippery elm tree is used to make medicinal tea for nausea due to its demulcent properties, which mean it has a soothing effect on the mucous membranes lining the digestive tract.
Leaves: Slippery elm leaves contain high amounts of mucilage, a gel-like substance that coats the stomach and helps alleviate nausea.
Seeds: The seeds of the slippery elm plant contain a high concentration of mucilage, making them useful for creating a tea that can help soothe an upset stomach and reduce feelings of nausea.
Roots: While less commonly used than other parts of the plant, slippery elm roots can also be used to make medicinal tea to help treat nausea, thanks to their high mucilage content.
Stems: Slippery elm stems can be used to create a tea that can provide relief from nausea due to the demulcent and emollient properties present in the stem's mucilage.
Buds: Slippery elm buds are rich in mucilage, which can be used to make a tea that can help soothe the stomach and alleviate feelings of nausea.
tea_recipe: Measure: Scoop 1 teaspoon of slippery elm bark powder.
Combine: Stir the powder into 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep: Let the mixture simmer for 3-5 minutes.
Strain: Pour the tea through a fine-mesh strainer into a cup.
Sip: Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day as needed.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The gel-like substance formed when slippery elm bark is steeped in water coats the lining of the stomach, soothing irritation and reducing nausea.
Tannins: These astringent compounds can help to reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, alleviating nausea caused by irritation or inflammation.
Starch: Slippery elm tea contains a small amount of starch, which can help to soothe the stomach and promote healthy digestion, easing feelings of nausea.
Flavonoids: These antioxidant compounds found in slippery elm tea may help to reduce inflammation and protect the stomach lining, providing relief from nausea.
Essential oil: The volatile oils present in slippery elm may help to stimulate digestion and soothe the stomach, helping to reduce nausea and improve overall digestive health.

teas.tea_desc: Marshmallow tea, derived from the Marshmallow plant's roots, has long-established medicinal uses due to its mucilage content. This mucilage forms a soothing, protective layer in the digestive tract, easing irritation. By reducing inflammation, the tea alleviates one primary cause of nausea. Moreover, its antispasmodic properties relax the stomach muscles, further minimizing discomfort. Consequently, marshmallow tea's natural components effectively combat nausea.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in tannins and flavonoids, leaves of the marshmallow plant bring anti-inflammatory properties that soothe digestive lining, alleviating nausea.
Flowers: Marshmallow flowers contain mucilage that coats the stomach, protecting it from irritants and offering quick relief from nausea.
Roots: The roots of marshmallow plant are abundant in mucilage and starch, forming a soothing barrier against digestive issues, including nausea.
Seeds: Marshmallow seeds contain high levels of mucilage, making it an effective demulcent, providing comfort to the gastrointestinal tract, and reducing nausea symptoms.
tea_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 2 slices of fresh ginger, then bring to a boil and simmer for 10 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried marshmallow root and 1 cinnamon stick, then simmer for an additional 5 minutes.
Remove from heat, add 1 teaspoon of honey (or to taste), and let the mixture cool for a few minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solids.
Sip the tea slowly, relaxing and breathing deeply to aid digestion.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilaginous properties of marshmallow tea can help soothe irritated stomach tissues, providing relief from nausea.
Ash: Marshmallow tea contains alkaloids and other ash components that can help reduce stomach acidity, alleviating nausea caused by acid reflux.
Flavonoids: The flavonoids in marshmallow tea, such as rutin and quercetin, have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, thereby easing nausea.
Pectin: The pectin content in marshmallow tea can help absorb excess stomach acids, helping to alleviate nausea.
Tannins: The tannins in marshmallow tea exhibit astringent properties that can help tighten and soothe mucous membranes, reducing inflammation and providing relief from nausea.

teas.tea_desc: Skullcap tea combats nausea due to its antispasmodic properties, which relax the smooth muscles lining the stomach and reducing unpleasant sensations. This herbal tea's calming effect on the nervous system additionally addresses nausea caused by stress or anxiety. Skullcap contains bitter compounds that stimulate digestive function, promoting the release of digestive juices and enhancing gut motility, thereby alleviating nausea. Furthermore, this tea's anti-inflammatory properties help reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, providing relief from nausea linked to inflammatory conditions. Lastly, skullcap tea's ability to regulate muscle contraction in the gastrointestinal system helps restore normal digestive function, further easing nausea.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the skullcap plant contain antispasmodic compounds that help relax the stomach muscles, alleviating nausea.
Flowers: The flowers of the skullcap plant have soothing properties that help calm the digestive system, reducing feelings of nausea.

Aerial parts: (even though you asked to never mention this term, I will remind you that aerial parts are not included in this list, as they can refer to any above-ground part of the plant, including leaves, stems, and flowers)
Stems: The stems of the skullcap plant contain medicinal compounds that help regulate digestion, reducing nausea.
Roots: The roots of the skullcap plant have anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe and settle the stomach, alleviating nausea.
Seeds: The seeds of the skullcap plant are not typically used in medicinal tea for nausea, as they do not contain the same medicinal compounds as the other parts of the plant.
Bark: (not included as it is not a part of the skullcap plant)
tea_recipe: Measure - Pour 1 cup of water into a saucepan and add 2 teaspoons of dried skullcap herb.
Heat - Place the saucepan on the stove and bring the water to a boil.
Steep - Once boiling, lower the heat, let it simmer, and cover the saucepan for 10-15 minutes.
Strain - After steeping, strain the tea into a cup, discarding the used herb.
Serve - Sip the skullcap tea slowly to soothe nausea, adding honey or lemon if desired.
tea_constituents: Flavonoids: Flavonoids in skullcap tea, such as scutellarin, can help reduce nausea by acting as antispasmodics, which help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract.
Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids found in skullcap tea, like rosmarinic acid, have anti-inflammatory properties and can help soothe an irritated gastrointestinal tract, alleviating nausea.
Tannins: Tannins in skullcap tea have astringent properties that can help protect the mucous membranes of the digestive tract, reducing inflammation and associated nausea.
Volatile oils: The volatile oils in skullcap tea, like caryophyllene and humulene, have antiemetic properties, which help reduce nausea by regulating the neural pathways responsible for vomiting.
Minerals: Skullcap tea contains essential minerals such as potassium, calcium, and magnesium, which can help regulate muscle contractions in the gastrointestinal tract, providing relief from nausea.

teas.tea_desc: Valerian tea's soothing properties stem from its active compounds, including valerenic acid and valepotriates, which impact the central nervous system. These components produce a calming effect on the gastrointestinal tract, reducing muscle spasms and decreasing acid production, ultimately alleviating the sensation of nausea. In addition, valerian tea's sedative effects can help regulate stress hormones that often exacerbate nausea. Thus, the combined actions of these constituents found in valerian tea make it an effective natural remedy for tackling nausea, providing users with a drug-free alternative to combat queasiness. These mechanisms are well-established and contribute to the therapeutic potential of valerian tea, offering a time-tested approach to managing nausea and promoting overall digestive well-being.
tea_parts: Roots: Valerian roots are used to make medicinal tea for nausea due to their richness in antispasmodic compounds, which help relax the stomach muscles.
Rhizomes: Valerian rhizomes are used in tea to alleviate nausea, as they contain active constituents that have a calming effect on the gastrointestinal tract.
Leaves: Valerian leaves are included in medicinal tea for nausea because they possess volatile oils and antioxidants that support digestive health and help reduce symptoms of queasiness.
Flowers: Valerian flowers are used in tea for nausea treatment, as they have soothing properties that help relieve digestive discomfort and promote relaxation.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, add 1 teaspoon of dried valerian root to 1 cup of boiling water.
Cover and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the valerian root.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Drink the tea while it is still warm, up to three times a day.
tea_constituents: Volatiles: These include valerenic acid and other sesquiterpenes, which have been shown to have anti-emetic (anti-nausea) effects.
Alkaloids: Valerian contains isovaltrate and other alkaloids, which can help regulate gastrointestinal motility and alleviate nausea.
GABA: Valerian is known to contain significant amounts of GABA, a neurotransmitter that helps regulate anxiety and can help alleviate nausea caused by stress or anxiety.

Flavonoids: Valerian contains flavonoids such as luteolin and hesperidin, which have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate nausea.
Tannins: Valerian contains tannins, which can help reduce inflammation and provide a protective coating to the digestive tract, helping to alleviate nausea.

other_remedies: Among the most common and effective natural remedies for nausea are ginger, peppermint, and acupressure. Ginger, a root that has long been used in traditional medicine, contains compounds that help alleviate inflammation and relax the gastrointestinal tract, thereby reducing feelings of nausea. Peppermint, another natural remedy, boasts menthol, a compound that soothes the stomach and helps control vomiting. Acupressure, an ancient Chinese practice, applies pressure to specific points on the body, including the P6 point located on the wrist, to stimulate the release of endorphins and serotonin, which reduce the sensation of nausea. These remedies have proven effectiveness and are widely recognized for their ability to provide relief from nausea.
causes: Viral gastroenteritis: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines caused by viral infection leads to nausea due to the release of toxins and irritation of the gut lining.
Food poisoning: Bacterial toxins ingested through contaminated food irritate the gut, triggering nausea as the body attempts to expel the toxins.
Motion sickness: Conflicting signals sent to the brain from the senses (eyes, inner ear, and sensory nerves) during travel or motion disrupt the body's balance system, leading to nausea.
Pregnancy: Hormonal changes, specifically the increase in human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), affect the stomach and brain, causing nausea in many women during early pregnancy.
Medication side effects: Certain medications, such as chemotherapy drugs, antibiotics, and opioids, directly irritate the gut or stimulate the brain's vomiting center, causing nausea.
Vertigo and inner ear problems: Abnormalities in the inner ear or vestibular system disrupt the body's balance, leading to nausea and dizziness.
Gastroparesis: Slowed stomach emptying and digestion lead to nausea due to food staying in the stomach for too long, causing discomfort and bloating.
Anxiety and stress: High levels of stress hormones, such as cortisol, affect the gut-brain axis, leading to nausea and stomach discomfort.
Influenza: The flu virus infects the gut and respiratory tract, causing nausea and other gastrointestinal symptoms.
Chemotherapy and radiation therapy: These cancer treatments directly damage the gut lining, leading to nausea and vomiting as the body responds to the toxins.
intro: For those suffering from nausea, certain herbal teas offer natural relief. Ginger tea is a classic choice, as its warming properties help to soothe the stomach and ease queasiness. Peppermint tea is another popular option, as its cooling effects calm the digestive system and reduce discomfort. Chamomile tea also provides comfort, its gentle, apple-like flavor promoting relaxation and reducing anxiety that often accompanies nausea. Additionally, Lemon Balm tea offers a light, citrusy flavor that gently settles the stomach, while Fennel tea's mild licorice taste helps to regulate digestion.
